coredata.dc:description: Formulating an effective delivery system that facilitates bioactive factors/drugs penetrating dense cartilage surface structure to reach deep zones and repair cartilage injury is a critical and unresolved problem. In this work, carbon quantum dots prepared from m-phenylenediamine (m-CQDs) were used to develop a novel multifunctional delivery system for chondrocytes and cartilage. The proposed delivery system exhibited quick transfection ability (30 min), high transfection effectiveness (exceeding 90%), excellent biocompatibility (92% cell activity) and stable photoluminescence in chondrocytes. Remarkably, the system could effectively reach the chondrocytes in the deep cartilage zones due to its small diameter (4–5 nm). To determine the deliverability of m-CQDs in chondrocytes and cartilage tissues, we prepared m-CQD-HA (i.e., m-CQD carrying oligomeric HA) and evaluated the effect of m-CQD-HA on osteoarthritis chondrocyte model and cartilage tissue. The results showed that m-CQD-HA provided a better treatment effect than HA. In summary, m-CQD is an excellent multifunctional delivery vehicle with small size, high cellular uptake efficiency, exceptional biocompatibility, and integrated imaging function. Most importantly, m-CQDs could help bioactive factors penetrate the dense structure of cartilage surface layer into deep cartilage zones, substantially increasing their therapeutic effect.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of m-CQD synthesis. A, Schematic of m-CQDs synthesis. B, TEM image of m-CQDs (Scale bar = 100 nm); insert: m-CQD particle diameter. C, Fluorescence emission of m-CQDs; insert: Images of aqueous solutions of m-CQDs.

Transfection rate of m-CQDs in chondrocyte cell line C28/I2 approached 100%. A, Identification results of chondrocyte phenotype by immunocytochemistry analysis. B, Real-time live-cell imaging monitoring images of C28/I2 after incubation with medium containing m-CQDs for 30 min under excitation at 488 nm. C, Representative confocal microscopy images of m-CQDs, GFP-Ad., and GFP plasmid transfection groups for 24 h. D, Representative transfection rate of above transfection groups for 24 h by flow cytometry; data were quantified by average transfection rate.

m-CQDs has better biocompatibility than Ad., and plasmid in chondrocyte cell line C28I2. Representative cell viability results of m-CQD, GFP-Ad., and GFP plasmid transfection groups for 24 h by flow cytometry; data were quantified by average cell viability.

Low-concentration m-CQDs have excellent biocompatibility in chondrocyte cell line C28/I2. A, Representative cell survival rates of different concentrations of m-CQD transfection groups by RTCA assay for 48 h. B, RTCA data were quantified by average cell index (n = 3). C, Representative confocal microscopy images of different concentrations of m-CQD transfection groups for 48 h. D, Data of image C were quantified. E, Representative protein expressions of MMP-13 (red band) and COL-2 (green band) in different concentration transfection groups for 48 h by Western blot. Quantified gray value ratio of MMP-13/GAPDH (n = 3). Quantified gray value ratio of COL-2/GAPDH (n = 3). F, Representative cell viability of different concentrations of m-CQD transfection groups for 48 h by flow cytometry; the average cell survival rate of the above groups was quantified (n = 3).

m-CQDs could reach cells residing inside deep zones of human cartilage explants through the dense surface. A–C, Protocol of human cartilage explant culture. D–E, Representative confocal microscopy images across a section of human cartilage explants incubated with 10% FBS with or without m-CQDs for 48 h; boxes a, b, and c show surface, middle, and deep layers of cartilage. F, Average m-CQD transfection rate of surface, middle, and deep layers of human cartilage was quantified (n = 3) per group. G, Schematic of indentation principle using a fiber-optic probe. H, Representative load-displacement curve of the last indentation. I, Representative time data tab. J, Quantified average Young’s modulus of control and m-CQD transfection groups (n = 5 per group).

m-CQDs successfully penetrate the dense surface, and no local or systemic toxicity effects were observed. A, Representative fluorescence images of rat knee joint during 0–48 h after intra-articular injection of m-CQDs. B, Small-animal live-imaging system data were quantified by average ROI (n = 3). C, Representative confocal microscopy images across a section of rat articular cartilage with or without m-CQD intra-articular injection for 24 and 48 h; m-CQDs were detected by green fluorescence. D, Representative fluorescence images across the section of rat cartilage tissuse received m-CQD intra-articular injection for 24 h; boxes 1–8 show muscle, synovium, meniscus, cartilage, subchondral bone, trabecular bone, epiphyseal, and marrow cavity; m-CQDs were detected by green fluorescence and DAPI for cell nuclei. E, Representative Safranin O staining images of rat cartilage tissue with or without m-CQD injection for 48 h. F, Levels of key indicators (AST, ALT, AST/ALT, TBIL, DBIL, IBIL, urea, and Cr. in serum) for evaluating liver and kidney functions were detected 48 h after injection.

Overview of m-CQD-HA synthesis and characteristics. A, Schematic of m-CQD-HA synthesis. B, Representative confocal microscopy images of m-CQD-HA in C28/I2 for 30 min. C, Representative cell survival rate of different concentrations of m-CQD-HA transfection group for 72 h by RTCA assay. D, RTCA data quantified by average cell index (n = 3). E, Representative cell viability of different concentrations of m-CQD-HA transfection groups by flow cytometry.

m-CQD-HA improved OA chondrocyte survival rate and biological function more effectively than HA. A, Average concentration of HA in different concentrations of m-CQD-HA groups by ELISA kits (n = 3). G: Representative cell survival rate of control, IL-1, m-CQD-HA, and HA groups for 72 h by RTCA assay. H: RTCA data quantified by average cell index (n = 3). I: Representative cell viability of above groups (same as the image in G) for 72 h by flow cytometry; data were quantified by average live cell percentage (n = 3). J: Representative protein expressions of MMP-13 (red band) and COL-2 (green band) in above groups for 72 h by Western blot; gray value ratio of MMP-13/GAPDH and COL-2/GAPDH were quantified (n = 3).

m-CQD-HA improved OA cartilage tissue biological and mechanical properties more effectively than HA. A: Representative confocal microscopy images across a section of human cartilage explants incubated with or without m-CQD-HA for 72 h; boxes a, b, and c show surface, middle, and deep layers of cartilage, respectively. B: Representative Safranin O staining results of human cartilage explants incubated with 10% FBS, m-CQD-HA, or HA for 72 h; average Mankin’s score was quantified (n = 5 per group). C: Average Young’s modulus of groups was quantified (n = 6 per group).

head.ce:title: Novel multifunctional delivery system for chondrocytes and articular cartilage based on carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Formulating an effective delivery system that facilitates bioactive factors/drugs penetrating dense cartilage surface structure to reach deep zones and repair cartilage injury is a critical and unresolved problem. In this work, carbon quantum dots prepared from m-phenylenediamine (m-CQDs) were used to develop a novel multifunctional delivery system for chondrocytes and cartilage. The proposed delivery system exhibited quick transfection ability (30 min), high transfection effectiveness (exceeding 90%), excellent biocompatibility (92% cell activity) and stable photoluminescence in chondrocytes. Remarkably, the system could effectively reach the chondrocytes in the deep cartilage zones due to its small diameter (4–5 nm). To determine the deliverability of m-CQDs in chondrocytes and cartilage tissues, we prepared m-CQD-HA (i.e., m-CQD carrying oligomeric HA) and evaluated the effect of m-CQD-HA on osteoarthritis chondrocyte model and cartilage tissue. The results showed that m-CQD-HA provided a better treatment effect than HA. In summary, m-CQD is an excellent multifunctional delivery vehicle with small size, high cellular uptake efficiency, exceptional biocompatibility, and integrated imaging function. Most importantly, m-CQDs could help bioactive factors penetrate the dense structure of cartilage surface layer into deep cartilage zones, substantially increasing their therapeutic effect.

ce:section.ce:para: Articular cartilage plays a critical role in maintaining frictionless movement throughout an individual’s lifetime by bearing repetitive compressive stress. However, it also frequently sustains damage due to trauma, aging, and disease [1,2]. Furthermore, various injuries could increase the levels of synovial cytokines that promote tissue degradation, ultimately causing osteoarthritis (OA) [3,4]. OA is associated with joint stiffness, pain, significant disability, and loss of productivity, which is considered one of the main causes of disability among middle-aged people and the elderly [5]. To date, no effective treatment is available for cartilage injury and OA [6,7]. More recently, researchers have discovered that bioactive factors, such as proteins, nucleic acids, carbohydrates, or molecules (synthetic or biologic), can repair cartilage injury or delay its process via attenuating catabolism and eliciting anabolic response in host tissues or cells, particularly in its early stage when the cartilage structure has not been destroyed and the surface layer is intact [8,9]. However, both oral and topical administration results in inefficient delivery of the drug to the joint [10–12]. One of the key reasons is that bioactive factors can not penetrate the dense cartilage surface structure into deep zones to repair cartilage injury [13,14].

The delivery system can transport bioactive factors or drugs to the target, thus, improving their bioavailability at the target site. An ideal delivery system must have a high transfection rate, excellent cell compatibility, facile surface modification and integrated imaging function to allow targeting [15,16]. Such a system can facilitate safer, more accurate, and more effective disease treatment [17]. For articular cartilage, the ideal delivery system must have a preferred characteristic of small molecular size, which will help bioactive factors penetrate the dense cartilage surface structure into deep cartilage zones [18–20]. Many micro-carriers have been developed to formulate a more effective delivery system for the cartilage [21]. These include plasmid vector, adenovirus (Ad.) vector, polylactic–glycolic acid [22,23], micro-carriers and nanocarriers (e.g., gelatin, chitosan, polycaprolactone (PCL), polyanhydrides, and polyesteramides) [24,25], liposomes [26,27], micelles, and dendrimers [28,29]. However, all these micro-carriers have similar deficiencies: inadequate cell compatibility and low transfection efficiency. Moreover,they themselves have no imaging function, they become multifunctional system by combining the functional imaging units. Such a combination can evidently increase the size of the delivery system [17,30,31]. To overcome the foregoing problem, the use of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has attracted our interest. These CQDs are new nano-sized particle materials with unique characteristics. They are small in size (less than 10 nm), non-toxic, and inexpensive; they have excellent photoluminescence, high aqueous dispersibility, and facile synthesis methods; and they enable easy surface modification [32]. Accordingly, CQDs have been extensively applied to bio-imaging, bio-sensing, and chemical-sensing both in vitro and in vivo. They have also been employed for diagnostic purposes, drug and gene deliveries, and photodynamic and photothermal therapies [33–35]. However, to the best of our knowledge, CQDs have rarely been used to treat cartilage diseases.

In our previous study, we designed and prepared m-phenylenediamine-doped carbon quantum dots (m-CQDs) as a multifunctional delivery system for cancer treatment. The results showed that m-CQDs are approximately 3.8 nm in size and amino-abundant, rendering them easy to modify. Additionally, m-CQDs exhibit excellent cell compatibility and typical excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior; hence, they can be monitored by real-time imaging [36]. These observations indicate that m-CQD may be a prospective candidate for bio-imaging, drug delivery, and tracking and has potential use in biomedical areas. Most importantly, the small size of m-CQD may possibly enable it to penetrate the dense structure of the cartilage surface layer into deep cartilage zones, increasing the effectiveness of the bioactive factor. To verify the preceding hypothesis, in this study, we examined the transfection efficiency, biocompatibility, fluorescence behavior, metabolic behavior, permeability, and bioactive factor delivery ability of m-CQDs for chondrocytes and cartilage via in vitro and in vivo experiments.

L-cysteine and m-phenylenediamine (m-PD) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). C28/I2 cells were provided by the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Cartilage samples were obtained from “relatively normal” cartilage samples of the tibial plateau obtained during total knee arthroplasty. 10-week-old male SD rats (180–230 g) were obtained from the Experimental Animal Centre, Shanxi Medical University, China. Dulbecco's Modification of Eagle's Medium: Nutrient Mixture F-12 (DMEM/F12) medium and other reagents used for cell and cartilage tissues culture were purchased from Invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water purified by a Millipore Milli-Q system (Billerica, MA, USA) was used throughout the experiments. Green fluorescent protein (GFP) Ad. empty vector and GFP plasmid empty vector were constructed by Genechem Co (China). lipofectamine 2000 was purchased from invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA). PE Annexin V and 7-AAD staining kits was purchased from BD Pharmingen™ (NJ Transit, USA). The total protein of chondrocytes was isolated and quantified using the BCA Protein Assay Reagent Kit (Boster Biological Technology Co., Ltd., China). All primary antibodies used in this study were purchased from Abcam (USA), all secondary antibodies were purchased from LI-COR (USA), other reagents used for western blotting were purchased from Boster Biological Technology Co., Ltd.(China). The reagents used for Safranin O staining were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (USA). The human ELISA kit for HA was purchased from Cloud-Clone Corp.(China).

The morphological characteristics of m-CQDs were measured by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, TEM (JEOL-2100F, Tokyo, Japan) and Atomic Force Microscope, AFM (NX10, Park, Korea). The absorption spectra were obtained from a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (METASH UV8000, Shanghai, China). The fluorescence spectra were evaluated using an FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Livingston, UK). Cell transfection rates and survival rates were evaluated using a flow cytometer (CyFlow® Cube 6, Partec, Germany). The cell survival rates analysis was detected by a real-time cell electronic sensing system (ACEA Biosciences, Inc.). Representative confocal microscopy images of cells were recorded by the living cell real time monitoring system (TCS-SP8, Leica, Germany). The western blotting results were visualized using Odyssey® CLx (LI-COR, USA), and band densities were quantified using Empiria Studio Analysis Software (LI-COR, USA). Fluorescent signals in cartilage tissues of SD rats were dynamically monitored using a small-animal fluorescence live-imaging system (Caliper IVIS Lumina, Perkin Elmer, USA). 6-μm thick sections were trimmed using a rotary microtome (LeicaRM2125, Leica Microsystems Ltd., Wetzlar, Germany). This study determined the micro-elasticity of human cartilage samples using a novel nanoindentation instrument (Piuma Nanoindenter, Optics11, Amsterdam, The Netherlands).

For the experiment, 800 mg of L-cysteine and 500 mg of m-phenylenediamine (m-PD) were dissolved in 30 mL of double-distilled water. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred into a 50-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 8 h. The resulting mixture was then removed using solvents and further vacuum-dried. Finally, pure m-CQDs were obtained and stored at 4 °C for further use ( Fig. 1A).

Purified and frozen-dried m-CQDs were obtained from the prepared 5 mg/mL solution of hyaluronic acid (HA). According to the system with a 5-mL volume, HA and m-CQDs were mixed at a mass ratio of 1:4. Then, a 0.1 mol/L hydrochloric acid solution was used to adjust the pH level of the system to 4.8. Moreover, 20 times more excess 1-(3-(dimethylamino)-propyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride were added to the mixture. The pH level of the entire reaction system was maintained at 4.8 for 2 h, and the reaction was terminated with the pH level adjusted to 7.0. The solution obtained from the reaction was further subjected to dialysis using a 3500 Da dialysis bag, and m-CQD-HA was frozen-dried and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C (Fig. 7A).

Chondrocyte cell line C28/I2 was seeded in a glass-bottom dish at a concentration of 2 × 106 cells/dish. At 90% confluence, the cells were treated with m-CQDs (0.05 μg/mL), green fluorescent protein (GFP) Ad. empty vector (1 × 106 PFU/mL), GFP plasmid empty vector (4 ug), or Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium:: Nutrient Mixture F-12 (DMEM/F12) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). In the m-CQD transfection group, 10 uL m-CQD (10 μg/mL) was dissolved in 2 mL of DMEM/F12 containing 10% FBS; 0.05 μg/mL of m-CQD mix was obtained to treat cultured C28/I2 in vitro. In the GFP Ad. empty vector transfection group, 20 uL of GFP Ad. empty vector (1 × 109 PFU/mL) was dissolved in 2 mL of DMEM/F12 containing 10% fetal FBS; a 1 × 106 PFU/mL GFP Ad. empty vector mix was obtained to treat cultured C28/I2 in vitro for 48 h. In the GFP plasmid empty vector transfection group, the cells were cultured overnight under serum-free conditions. Subsequently, they were treated and transfected with plasmid using lipofectamine 2000 according to the manufacturer’s instructions. The transfected cells were incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 48 h. The dish was then placed on a confocal scanner box with a culture system maintained at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Wide-field fluorescence images were also acquired. Time-lapse image sequences of the m-CQD transfection group were acquired for up to 30 min at 2-min intervals. Representative confocal microscopy images of cells were obtained 48 h after transfection. Cell transfection and survival rates were evaluated using a flow cytometer. The immunocytochemistry analysis of chondrocyte phenotype was implemented as previously described using anti-type I and anti-type-II collagen monoclonal antibodies [37]. The cells were positive for type-II collagen and negative for type I collagen ( Fig. 2A). To further study the biocompatibility of m-CQDs, chondrocyte cell line C28/I2 was seeded in 35 mm culture dishes at a concentration of 2 × 106 cells/dish. Moreover, 100, 50, 10, and 5 uL m-CQDs (10 μg/mL) were dissolved in 2 mL of DMEM/F12 containing 10% FBS to derive an m-CQD mix (0.5, 0.25, 0.05, and 0.025 μg/mL). At 90% confluence, the cells were treated with different concentrations of m-CQD mix. All experiments were repeated thrice.

In real-time cell analysis, a complete medium (100 µL) containing 2 × 103–4 × 103 cells was loaded in each well of the 96-well plate. The plate was incubated for a minimum of 30 min in a humidified (37 °C) 5% CO2 incubator and then inserted into a real-time cell electronic sensing system; cell survival rate was monitored for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability measurement by flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: Chondrocytes (C28/I2) were seeded in six-well culture plates at 80–90% confluence. Moreover, the cells were subjected to different treatments according to specific experiment requirements. These treatments include 1. DMEM/F12 containing 10% fetal FBS as control; 2. m-CQDs (0.05 μg/mL), GFP Ad. empty vector (1 × 106 PFU/mL), and GFP plasmid empty vector (4 ug); 3. different concentrations of m-CQD (0.025, 0.05, 0.25, and 0.5 μg/mL); 4. different concentrations of m-CQD-HA (0.025, 0.05, 0.25, and 0.5 μg/mL); 5. IL-1 (10 ng/mL), IL-1 +HA (8 ng/mL), and IL-1 +m-CQD-HA (0.025 μg/mL). According to the manufacturer’s instructions, the viability of cells was detected after 48 h by PE Annexin V and 7-AAD staining kits. Stock solutions of 7-AAD and PE Annexin V were prepared. Live (orange) and dead (red) cells were detected and the percentages of live and dead cells were quantified by a flow cytometer. The experiment was repeated thrice.

The total protein of chondrocytes was isolated and quantified using the BCA Protein Assay Reagent Kit. Moreover, 15 μg of total protein were electrophoresed under reducing conditions by 10% sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis. After electrophoresis, the proteins were transferred onto a nitrocellulose membrane and probed with primary antibody against COL-2, matrix metalloproteinase-13 (MMP-13) or glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH). The primary antibody was diluted 1:1000 in phosphate buffer saline-tween (PBS-T) containing 1% bovine serum albumin. Subsequently, the secondary antibodies of IRDye® 800CW goat anti-mouse or IRDye® 680RD goat anti-rabbit were diluted at a ratio of 1:5000 in PBS-T. The immunoreactive proteins were visualized using Odyssey® CLx, and band densities were quantified using Empiria Studio Analysis Software.

Cartilage samples were obtained from “relatively normal” cartilage samples of the tibial plateau obtained during total knee arthroplasty. The cartilage samples were then cut into 4–5-mm3 pieces (each weighing 6–9 mg) using a scalpel and cultured in DMEM/F12 supplemented with 10% FBS (Fig. 5 A–C). The collected explants were randomly subdivided into three groups: treatment with DMEM/F12 supplemented with 10% FBS, 0.025 μg/mL of m-CQD, and 0.025 μg/mL of m-CQD-HA. After 48 h of treatment without removing the reagent, a part of each sample was cut off from the subchondral bone for nanoindentation tests, a part of each sample was for Safranin O staining, and confocal microscopy image determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transfection effects of m-CQDs in vivo: a pilot study
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the in vivo effects of m-CQDs, m-CQD (10 μg/mL) was injected into the right knee joint of 10-week-old male Sprague Dawley rats (n = 18), whereas the control rats were injected with 1 × PBS; the volume of each injection was 40 µL. Fluorescent signals were dynamically monitored using a small-animal fluorescence live-imaging system until the positive signal disappeared (n = 6). The animals were euthanized 24 and 48 h after treatment. The cartilage tissues were collected to observe the distribution of m-CQD by fluorescence microscopy. The serum was collected to evaluate the liver and kidney functions of rats.

Forty-eight hours after culture, human cartilage explants were fixed in 10% formalin for 72 h. The specimens were decalcified in a Richman–Gelfand–Hill solution, and embedded in a single block of Paraplast X-tra. Blocks were trimmed to expose the tissue using a rotary microtome, 10 adjacent sections were collected at intervals of 0, 100, and 200 µm. Two serial 6-μm thick sections from each interval were stained with Safranin O. In the in vivo study, the femurs and tibiae were hemisected in the coronal plane, and each half was embedded in a single block of Paraplast X-tra. Blocks were trimmed using the same method described above, and the 6-μm thick sections were then stained with Safranin O.

Nanoindentation is widely applied for detecting the mechanical properties of tissues or materials at small scales. This study determined the micro-elasticity of human cartilage samples using a novel nanoindentation instrument. The Piuma interferometer contains a laser source whose light is coupled into an optical fiber. The light is trapped inside the optical fiber by the refractive index difference between the core and cladding. As the fiber terminates in the probe, a sudden change in the refractive index (fiber-to-air/medium) occurs, causing the interface to reflect a part of the light into the fiber. The remaining light is coupled to the air or medium and reflected by the next interface (the cantilever). This reflection is also coupled back into the fiber. The two back-propagated waves interfere with each other, and a photodiode detects the resulting intensity. In the case of a shift between the two reflecting interfaces, the interference also changes, resulting in a higher or lower total intensity at the end of the photodetector. The continuous shift of these two interfaces can be observed from the sinusoidal intensity pattern at the end of the photodetector (Fig. 5G). A probe with a 5.17 N/m spring constant and 25 µm spherical indentation tip was used. During indentation, the spherical tip was brought in contact with the sample surface; load–indentation and load–time data were recorded. Probe displacement and probe displacement velocities were set to 10 µm and 18 µm/s, respectively. Young’s modulus was derived from the load–indentation curves (Fig. 5, H and I) using the Hertz model. This model was applied to the loading dataset corresponding to 80% of the maximum load point; five unique points were measured per sample.
To estimate HA levels in m-CQD-HA agents, a commercially available human ELISA kit for HA was used. Each sample was duplicated for the procedure, and the manufacturer’s protocol was observed.

All experiments were performed in triplicate. Data were shown as the mean value ± standard error of the mean and statistically analyzed by a two-sided Student’s t-test; P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS software for Windows, version 24.0 (SPSS Inc., Chicago, Ill., USA).

The characteristics of m-CQDs are shown in Fig. 1A. They were well dispersed with spherical shape and had a uniform size in the range 4–5 nm, which approximated the expected values (Fig. 1B). We further provided the AFM topography image assay of m-CQDs, results showed that the average height of m-CQDs was 6 nm, which was consistent with TEM results (Fig. S5). Moreover, fluorescent emission peaks were virtually fixed at approximately 530 nm with an excitation wavelength of 460 nm (Fig. 1C). And our previous study showed that the fluorescence stability of m-CQDs under different conditions: pH values, UV irradiation time, storage time, and different batches were satisfactory [36]. The results indicated that the m-CQDs were excellently dispersed and small in size. They also had stable photoluminescence, which was crucial for nanomaterials to migrate to chondrocytes cultured in vitro and reside in the deep cartilage zone. Additionally, the surface functional groups of the m-CQDs was detected by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in our previous study, and the results showed that the m-CDs contained carboxyl and amino groups on their surface and good doping of S with the significant characteristics [36]. Abundant surface functional groups are the basis for its connection with bioactive factors. However, a further in-depth study is necessary to explore the transfection effectiveness, biocompatibility, fluorescence behavior, metabolic behavior, and penetrability of m-CQDs for cartilages and chondrocytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effectiveness of m-CQD transfection in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: To detect the transfection effectiveness of m-CQDs in chondrocytes cultured in vitro, we further studied the characteristics of m-CQDs in the C28/I2 cell line. First, cellular immunofluorescence analyses were performed to identify the chondrocyte phenotype of C28/I2 cell line by detecting the protein expression of type I collagen (Col-1, fibrous cartilage-related protein) and type-II collagen (Col-2, hyaline-cartilage specific protein). The high level of Col-2 in chondrocytes indicates that the cells are in an excellent functional state [38]. The results showed that the C28/I2 cells were positive for Col-2 and negative for Col-1 staining (Fig. 2A). To observe the transfection effectiveness, the transfection process of m-CQDs was dynamically monitored. The findings indicated that virtually all chondrocytes were transfected in the m-CQD group in 30 min with excitable and stable fluorescent signals (Fig. 2B).

Plasmid DNA transfection and Ad. DNA transfection are two effective bioactive molecule delivery systems commonly used in scientific research. In this work, the transfection rates of m-CQDs, empty Ad., and empty plasmid in the C28/I2 cell line were detected by confocal microscopy 24 h after transfection. The results indicated that the transfection rate of m-CQD was higher than those of Ad. and plasmid (Fig. 2C). Flow cytometry analysis confirmed that the effectiveness of m-CQD, empty Ad., and empty plasmid groups 24 h after transfection were 97.16%, 81.62%, and 31.23%, respectively (Fig. 2D). All these findings revealed that compared with Ad. and plasmid, m-CQD was a more excellent multifunctional delivery system because of its unique characteristics, such as the small size (4–5 nm), higher transfection efficiency, faster transfection speed, and excitable and stable fluorescent signals.

To determine the biocompatibility of m-CQDs, C28/I2 chondrocytes were incubated with m-CQDs, empty Ad., and plasmid. The cell survival rate was detected by flow cytometry analysis 24 h after transfection. The analysis results showed that the cell survival rates of m-CQDs, empty Ad., and plasmid group were 92.73%, 75.67%, and 52.20%, respectively ( Fig. 3). To further study the biocompatibility of m-CQDs in chondrocytes, C28/I2 chondrocytes were incubated with different doses of m-CQDs for 48 h; then, the cell survival rate was detected by RTCA assay. We found that more than 90% of the cells were live in the normal-control and low-concentration m-CQD transfected groups (0.025 and 0.05 μg/mL, respectively). However, the live cell counts of high-concentration m-CQD transfected groups (0.25 and 0.5 μg /mL) were significantly reduced ( Fig. 4, A and B), as confirmed by confocal microscopy and western blot assay results. The confocal microscopy images showed that 48 h after transfection, the transfection rate in low-concentration transfection groups of m-CQDs was approximately 95%, whereas the cells had intact cell morphology and a high cell survival rate. However, with the increase in transfection concentration, the cell survival rates significantly decreased in the high-concentration m-CQDs groups (Fig. 4, C and D). To confirm the chondrocyte function, we assessed COL-2 and hypertrophy chondrocyte specific protein MMP-13 48 h after transfection. The results showed that COL-2 protein expression decreased with the increase in m-CQD concentration, and MMP-13 protein expression increased gradually (Fig. 4E). Moreover, in the low-concentration m-CQD groups (especially in the 0.025 μg/mL group), the COL-2 protein level increased, and the MMP-13 protein level significantly decreased than those in the control group (Fig. 4E). This indicated that the biocompatibility in the low-concentration m-CQD groups was better, as further confirmed by flow cytometry (Fig. 4 F).

The results showed that the high concentration (0.5 μg/mL) of m-CQDs could significantly decrease the cell survival rate (Fig. 4). To further verify this conclusion, relatively normal human cartilage explants (Mankin score: 0–2) were cultured with or without 0.5 μg/mL m-CQDs. Safranin O staining and biological nanoindentation assay were used to evaluate the biomechanical properties of cartilage tissues. Safranin O staining is employed to detect the proteoglycan content in cartilage tissues: the redder the color, the higher the proteoglycan content. A biological nanoindentation assay could reflect the mechanical properties of cartilage tissue by measuring the tissue’s elastic index. The results showed that the cartilage proteoglycan expression and mechanical properties in the high-concentration m-CQD group were significantly reduced than those in the control group (S. 1). This indicated that the high concentration (0.5 μg/mL) of m-CQDs was toxic to chondrocytes and cartilage.

Although CQDs are excellent photothermal agents, their most common toxic side effect is photothermal toxicity [39,40]. The photothermal effect refers to the outcome in which the conduction band electrons of plasmonic nanoparticles irradiated with laser light undergo synchronized oscillations resulting in either absorption or scattering of applied light. The absorbed light is converted into heat, which irreversibly damages surrounding cells and tissue [41]. To confirm whether photothermal toxicity is among the reasons for m-CQD toxicity, C28/I2 chondrocytes were incubated with concentrated m-CQDs (0.025 and 0.5 μg/mL) at different fluorescence intensities (571, 200, and 150 a.u. (absorbance unit)). Cell survival rate was monitored in real-time 20 h after transfection at 15-min intervals using a live-cell imaging system. The results indicated that the rate of cell death in the high-concentration group gradually decreased with the decrease of fluorescence intensity. Cell death caused by the photothermal toxicity of m-CQDs in the low-concentration group (0.025 μg/mL) was insignificant (S. 2). The results suggested that photothermal toxicity might be among the most important reasons why the high concentration of m-CQDs led to chondrocyte death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effectiveness of m-CQD transfection in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: To detect the penetration capacity and biocompatibility of m-CQDs in vivo, relatively normal human cartilage explants (approximately 4–5 mm3 per piece) were treated with 10% FBS or 0.025 μg/mL m-CQD. After 48 h in culture and without removing the reagent, the cartilage tissue blocks embedded by paraffin were cut to 6 µm thick sections for confocal microscopy image determination. The results showed that the transfection rates of m-CQDs in the surface, middle, and deep layers of human cartilage explants all approach 100%. This meant that m-CQDs could reach the cells inside the deep zones of human cartilage explants by successfully penetrating the dense surface ( Fig. 5, D–F). Then, the mechanical characteristics of cartilage explants were determined using a bio-nanoindentation instrument. The Young’s modulus values showed no significant difference between the normal control and m-CQD transfection groups (0.025 μg/mL). The nanoindentation results indicated that the use of 0.025 μg/mL m-CQD yield satisfactory biocompatibility (Fig. 5, H–J).

To test the duration of m-CQDs in rat knee joints, m-CQD (10 μg/mL, 40 uL) and PBS were injected into the knee joints of SD rats in our pilot study. The fluorescent signal in the rat knee joint was dynamically monitored using a small-animal fluorescence live-imaging system at a wavelength of 480 nm. We found that fluorescence could be detected as early as 4 h after the injection of 53.3 ± 2.07 nmol. The fluorescence intensity reached its climax (143.21 ± 6.67 nmol) after 24 h. Subsequently, the region of interest (ROI) value gradually decreased ( Fig. 6, A and B). The rats were euthanized after 24 and 48 h, and the knee joint cartilage specimens were further observed by confocal fluorescence microscopy. We could not detect fluorescent signals in the PBS-injected knee joint cartilage, whereas distinct green fluorescent signals were observed in the m-CQD-injected knee joint cartilage. The results showed that m-CQDs could penetrate the dense surface layer and reach the cells residing inside the deep cartilage zones (Fig. 6C). Additionally, the green positive signals were observed in the cells of synovial membranes, meniscus, subchondral bone, epiphyseal plate, and marrow cavity; however, they were not in the muscle and bone cells (Fig. 6D). The results indicated that m-CQDs might not only penetrate the surface layer into the cells of deep cartilage tissue but also reach the cartilage peripheral structures, such as bone and synovial tissues. It should be noted that OA is a disease involving the cartilage and synovial tissue around the cartilage, subchondral bone, and other tissues [42,43]; hence, the characteristics of m-CQDs are crucial for OA treatment.

To assess the toxicity of m-CQDs in vivo, Safranin O staining was used to observe the matrix synthesis function of chondrocytes 48 h after injection. The results indicated no significant difference between m-CQD-injected and PBS-injected knee joint cartilages (Fig. 6E). To evaluate the liver and kidney functions, the levels of glutamic-oxaloacetic transaminase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), AST/ALT, total bilirubin (TBIL), direct bilirubin (DBIL), indirect bilirubin (IBIL), urea, and creatinine (Cr.) in serum were detected using an automatic biochemical analyzer 48 h after injection. The results showed no difference between the control and m-CQD injection groups (Fig. 6F). These findings indicated no local or systemic toxicity effect of m-CQDs was observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of m-CQD-HA in chondrocytes and cartilage
ce:section.ce:para: To further validate the delivery ability of bioactive factors of m-CQD in chondrocytes and cartilage tissues, we selected oligomeric HA (oligo-HA) as the bioactive factor to link with m-CQDs. Oligo-HA is an HA whose molecular weight is < 10 kDa; due to this weight, it has exhibited various biological properties than HA with high molecular weight (>1000 kDa). There are a considerable amount of experimental evidences supporting the pro-proliferation effects of oligo-HA. Another studies report that oligo-HA can induce pro-inflammatory effects or trigger cell apoptosis. Oligo-HA certainly has an attractive molecular weight as a form of HA, and further research is required to uncover the role of this molecule in chondrocytes and cartilage [44–47]. Thus, in previous experiments, different concentrations (8 and 150 ng/mL) of oligo-HA (3 kD) were used to treat OA chondrocyte models induced by IL-1ß (S. 5). The cell survival rates of groups were detected by flow cytometry after 48 h, and the results showed that concentration might be the most important determinant of the protective or destructive effect of oligo-HA. Moreover, a low-concentration oligo-HA (8.0 ng/mL) could effectively increase live cell counts.

Based on the above results, m-CQD carrying oligo-HA (3 kD) was prepared, and its transfection efficiency, biocompatibility, and delivery effect in vitro and in vivo were evaluated. The cell transfection results showed that m-CQD-HA (0.025 μg/mL) could swiftly transfect chondrocytes cultured in vitro (30 min), and the transfection rate approached 100% with stable green fluorescent signals ( Fig. 7B). To detect the biocompatibility of different concentrations of m-CQD-HA in chondrocytes (0.025, 0.05, 0.25, and 0.5 μg/mL), an RTCA assay was performed for 72 h. The results showed that the survival rate of chondrocytes in the 0.5 μg/mL group was significantly reduced. The survival rates of chondrocytes transfected with other concentrations (0.025, 0.05, and 0.25 μg/mL) of m-CQD-HA were significantly higher than the control group (Fig. 7, C and D). This suggested that m-CQD-HA had satisfactory biocompatibility, as verified by the flow cytometry assay (Fig. 7E). To verify the effect of m-CQD-HA in chondrocytes, the drug loading of different concentrations of m-CQD-HA was detected using a human HA ELISA kit ( Fig. 8A). Meanwhile, IL-1 ß (1 ng/mL) was used to induce the OA cell model. Next, the cell model was treated with m-CQD-HA (0.025 μg/mL) or HA (8 ng/mL) for 72 h. The RTCA assay results showed that compared to HA treatment group, the cell survival rate of the m-CQD-HA treatment group was significantly increased (Fig. 8, B and C); this result was confirmed by flow cytometry assay (Fig. 8, D and E). Western blotting was applied to detect the protein expression of COL-2 and MMP-13 in chondrocytes. The results indicated that both HA and m-CQD-HA could effectively increase the protein level of COL-2, reduce the protein level of MMP-13, and improve chondrocyte function. However, m-CQD-HA was more effective than HA (Fig. 8F).

To further determine the delivery ability of m-CQDs in cartilage tissues, human cartilage explants with a Mankin’s score of 3–4 (early stage of OA) were selected for culture and randomly divided into control group, HA treatment group (8 ng/mL), and m-CQD-HA treatment group (0.025 μg/mL). After 72 h of treatment, confocal microscopy was utilized to observe the delivery effect of m-CQD-HA in human cartilage explants. Distinct green fluorescent signals were observed throughout the cartilage matrix of the m-CQD-HA group ( Fig. 9A). To evaluate the therapeutic effect, Safranin O staining and biological nanoindentation assay were performed. The results showed that both HA and m-CQD-HA could effectively increase the proteoglycan level and improve the mechanical properties of cartilage tissue(Fig. 9, B and C). Further, the treatment effect of m-CQD-HA was better than that of HA, suggesting that the multifunctional delivery system of m-CQDs could effectively improve the therapeutic effect of HA in cartilage tissue.

In summary, this study confirmed that m-CQD is an excellent multifunctional delivery system with a small size, high cellular uptake efficiency, excellent biocompatibility, and integrated imaging function. Most importantly, m-CQDs could deliver bioactive factors to penetrate the dense structure of the cartilage surface layer into deep cartilage zones and increase the bioactive factor’s effectiveness.

Nevertheless, m-CQD remains a perfect delivery system mainly limited by photothermal effect and rapid clearance. Our future study will be aimed at further optimizing m-CQDs considering their photothermal effect, capability in drug/bioactive factor delivery, retention, and release for chondrocytes and articular cartilage.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Li Guo received her Ph.D. degree in orthopedic from Shanxi Medical University, China, in 2020. Now she works in Shanxi Key Laboratory of Bone and Soft Tissue Injury Repair, Department of Orthopedics, Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, China. Her current research focuses on developing multifunctional CQDs for chondrocytes and cartilage, and targeting therapy in osteoarthritis.

Qianqian Duan received her Ph.D. degree in material fluorescence characteristics in 2013 from Harbin Institute of Technology, China. She is working in College of Information and Computer of Taiyuan University of Technology as a professor now. Her research mainly focuses on the synthesis, modification, and application of nanoparticles in biomedical and environmental fields.

Gaige Wu received his Bachelor in Medicine from Zhengzhou University,China, in 2018. Now he is pursuing M.D.degree from Shanxi Key Laboratory of Bone and Soft Tissue Injury Repair, Department of Orthopedics, Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, China. His current research focuses on Carbon dots carrying drugs to repair cartilage damage.

Boye Zhang received her M.D. degree in wireless sensor from Taiyuan University of Technology, China, in 2017. Now she is pursuing Ph.D. from MicroNano System Research Center of Taiyuan University of Technology. Her current research focuses on developing multicolored carbon dots for sensing applications.

Lingan Huang received his M.D. degree in orthopedic from Shanxi Medical University, China, in 2018. Now he is pursuing Ph.D. from Shanxi Key Laboratory of Bone and Soft Tissue Injury Repair, Department of Orthopedics, Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, China. His current research focuses on bone and soft tissue injury repair.

Juanjuan Xue received her Ph.D.degree in Microelectronics and Solid State Electronics from East China Normal University, China, in 2020. Now she works at the MicroNano System Research Center of Taiyuan University of Technology. Her current research focuses on developing nanomaterials for sensing applications.

Pengcui Li received her Ph.D. degree from Shanxi Medical University, China, in 2014. She now works in the Department of Orthopedics, the Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, China, mainly engaged in the research of articular cartilage injury and repair.

Shengbo Sang received his Ph.D. degree in nanoelectromechanical system in 2010 from Ilmenau University of Technology, Germany. He is currently a principal investigator (PI) of MicroNano System Research Center and a full professor of College of Information and Computer, Taiyuan University of Technology, China. His research focuses on the development of advanced electrochemical devices such as chemical sensors and biosensors.

Xiaochun Wei received his Ph.D. degree in orthopedic in 1998 from Linkoping University, Sweden. He is currently a principal investigator (PI) of Shanxi Key Laboratory of Bone and Soft Tissue Injury Repair, Department of Orthopedics, Second Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, China. His research focuses on the research of articular cartilage injury and repair.
